definition: The Fed's Reverse Repo Facility declined from ~$2.55T (peak Dec 2022) to <$200B (early 2026), marking a regime shift where QT now directly reduces bank reserves rather than draining the RRP buffer. This transition from Phase 1 (RRP-draining) to Phase 2 (reserve-draining) fundamentally changes QT's transmission.
evidence: Observable Fed balance sheet data showing RRP decline from $2.55T to <$200B
Fed's June 2024 taper (Treasury cap from $60B to $25B/month) as implicit acknowledgment
Money market funds shifted from RRP to T-bills during Phase 1, insulating bank reserves
All four debate pairs confirmed this as highest-conviction shared finding
open_questions: Was the smooth RRP drawdown driven by Fed management or Treasury bill issuance decisions?
Does the Fed have a proven playbook for the current reserve-draining phase?
